definition: Named or called, used chiefly in literary or playful writing.
origin: From Middle English icleped, past participle of clepen (to call, name), from Old English clēopan (to call).

rounds.label: Round 1 of 3 · Etymology
rounds.context: Where does Yclept come from? Match each root to its meaning.

pairs.b: To call out or name
To call or summon by name
That which has been named
An archaic or humorous way to say called

rounds.label: Round 2 of 3 · Definition
rounds.context: What does Yclept mean? Match each clue to the right answer.

pairs.b: Known by a particular name
Named
Literary, archaic, or tongue in cheek
Means a village called Bramblewick

rounds.label: Round 3 of 3 · Usage
rounds.context: How is Yclept used? Match each fragment to its correct completion.

pairs.b: Rode out at dawn to face the quest

pairs.a: A tavern yclept the Rusty Tankard
pairs.b: Stood at the corner of the high street

pairs.a: She wrote of a kingdom yclept
pairs.b: Avalon in her unfinished novel
Captain Flint with a theatrical bow

definition: In cosmology, hypothetical primordial matter from which the chemical elements were formed.
origin: From medieval Latin hylem, accusative of hyle (matter), from Greek hylē (wood, material, matter).

rounds.label: Round 1 of 3 · Etymology
rounds.context: Where does Ylem come from? Match each root to its meaning.

pairs.b: Wood, stuff, or raw material
Matter or substance in philosophy
A name for the hot dense soup before atoms formed
Big Bang nucleosynthesis and early universe physics

rounds.label: Round 2 of 3 · Definition
rounds.context: What does Ylem mean? Match each clue to the right answer.

pairs.b: Primordial cosmic matter
Theoretical physics and cosmology
An everyday word for ordinary stuff
Into the hydrogen and helium of the early universe

rounds.label: Round 3 of 3 · Usage
rounds.context: How is Ylem used? Match each fragment to its correct completion.

pairs.b: As the undifferentiated matter of the first moments
Through the first minutes after the Big Bang

pairs.a: In the textbook, ylem gives way
pairs.b: To plasma and then to neutral atoms
In historical accounts of cosmology

definition: At or to a distance, but within sight; over there.
origin: From Middle English yond, from Old English geond (beyond, over there).

rounds.label: Round 1 of 3 · Etymology
rounds.context: Where does Yonder come from? Match each root to its meaning.

pairs.b: Beyond or on the far side
At that place in the distance
Forms an adverb pointing to a visible distance
Poetic, regional, or deliberately old fashioned

rounds.label: Round 2 of 3 · Definition
rounds.context: What does Yonder mean? Match each clue to the right answer.

pairs.b: Over there, within view
Thither
Here
Are the ones you can see on the horizon

rounds.label: Round 3 of 3 · Usage
rounds.context: How is Yonder used? Match each fragment to its correct completion.

pairs.b: Marks the edge of the village

pairs.a: Walk toward the oak tree yonder
pairs.b: And wait by the gate

pairs.a: Smoke rose from the cottage yonder
pairs.b: Across the frost white field
Is still hidden by the morning mist

definition: Time long past, especially as remembered in story, song, or tradition.
origin: From Old English geara (of yore), related to gear (year, season).

rounds.label: Round 1 of 3 · Etymology
rounds.context: Where does Yore come from? Match each root to its meaning.

pairs.b: A year or season
Years ago or in former times
Of yore means in days long past
Literary, nostalgic, or folkloric

rounds.label: Round 2 of 3 · Definition
rounds.context: What does Yore mean? Match each clue to the right answer.

pairs.b: The distant past
Antiquity
The present day
Refers to a romanticised earlier age

rounds.label: Round 3 of 3 · Usage
rounds.context: How is Yore used? Match each fragment to its correct completion.

pairs.b: Rode out to defend the border lands
When the river ran full every spring
In the village harvest festival
Not to wander alone on the moor
